events.title: 2026 Needham High School Civics Showcase
events.description:
Event date: May 30, 2026 
Event Time: 02:00 PM - 03:30 PM
Location: 
1471 Highland Avenue
Needham, MA 02492
Description:
The League of Women Voters of Needham and the Needham High School Social Studies Department are co-sponsoring Students Advocate for Change, the Needham High School Civics Showcase, on Saturday, May 30 at 2-3:30 PM at Powers Hall, Needham Town Hall.All high school students in MA are required to select a state or local issue and prepare an advocacy plan. At the Showcase, more than 100 eleventh grade students in 50 groups will share their research, materials, and positions in a science fair type setting.The public is encouraged to come and talk with the next generation of policy advocates about the issues important to them. Visit www.lwv-needham.org for details.

events.title: Memorial Day Parade and Ceremony
events.description: Parade route and schedule 8:00-8:15am—Parade formation on South Ave., between Main Street and Washington St. 8:30am—Flag Ceremony at Moran Square at the Natick Center T-Station 8:45am—Parade marches to and around the Town Common Observances on the Common 9am Opening remarks at the Common John T. Martin, Veterans Services Officer Benediction- Chaplain, NSSC “National Anthem” by Natick High School Band Remarks from State Senator Karen Spilka’s office Remarks from State Representative David Linsky Remarks from Select Board Chair—Kathryn Coughlin Placing wreath at the Grand Army of Republic Monument by Scouts Keynote Speaker—Dave Josselyn, Vietnam Veteran “America the Beautiful” performed by Natick High Chorus Students “Taps” performed by Natick High School Band Parade forms up in front of the Common and marches: From East Central St. to West Central St. to Forest Ave. to Pond St. Parade marches and stops along the route for moment of silence and Taps, in honor of the fallen service members at: Old Dell Park Cemetery (163 Pond St.) St. Patrick’s Cemetery (180 Pond St.) Parade marches back to center of town and disbands All Veterans and Active Military are welcome to march in the parade. Please be advised that road closures will be in effect to accommodate parade traffic. In case of inclement weather, updates will be posted on the town website, and a decision regarding the parade will be made by 7am. If the parade needs to be moved indoors, the indoor location will be at the Natick Community Senior Center, located at 117 East Central St., Natick, at 9am. Thank you to Veterans Services Officer John Martin for this information.

events.title: TBElle | Laughing Through the Ages: Jewish Humor, Resilience and Survival (Hybrid)
events.description: This engaging course explores how Jewish communities have long turned to humor—rich with wit, irony, and a keen sense of the absurd—as a powerful way to endure hardship and persecution. Through key moments in Jewish history, we’ll discover how laughter has lived alongside suffering, fostering resilience, offering hope, and illuminating the depth and spirit of the Jewish experience. Dates: April 28, May 5, 12, 19, 26, June 2 Join us for one, two, or however many classes you’d like to participate in! Registration: This event is available in person and online. Please click the appropriate purple bar above to register. This program is a part of TBElle (Temple Beth Elohim Lifelong Learning Experience). For over ten years, TBElle has been a vibrant community where congregants learn and grow together. This relaxed and fun experience is led by you—our fellow TBE members—who are eager to share their knowledge, experiences, stories, and talents. Discover new passions, connect with others, and contribute your unique perspective. Come participate and enrich our shared journey!

events.description: In this course, we’ll explore pivotal statements from the Catholic Church and Protestant churches that dramatically reshaped Christianity’s view of Judaism—alongside the Jewish responses to these changes. Together, we’ll consider the lasting impact of these developments and how contemporary issues continue to influence them, including their role in shaping today’s interfaith dialogue. Dates: Tuesdays, May 19, 26, June 2 This course follows a sequential structure, but anyone is welcome to join at any point, or to drop in to any class. If you have questions regarding participation, please contact the program leader. Registration: This event is available in person and online. events.title: Drum Circle: Spring 2026 (In Person)
events.description: Drum Circle start date has been postponed to April 16. Join us for an exciting, joyful drum circle with our own Friday night service drummer, Noam Sender. A drum circle is a centuries-old tradition tapping into age-old wisdom. Drumming is known to reduce stress and promote a sense of well being and mental alertness. Seated in a circle, we will play beautiful, accessible and captivating rhythms from around the world. The drumming will be enhanced with familiar chants and melodies (singing is optional). We will deepen the drumming experience with brief mindfulness exercises, learning to center ourselves, relax and explore our authentic self, while building a deep sense of community and sharing our collective voice. No prior experience required. Drums are available if needed, but feel free to bring your own if you like. Class size is limited to between 6 and 8 participants so it is best to register soon! This course follows a sequential structure, and participants are encouraged to attend all sessions. If you are interested in joining once the course has begun, please contact the program leader. Dates & Time: April 16, 23, May 7, 14, 28, June 4 Fee: $180 Registration: This program is held in person. Please click the purple button above to register. events.title: Adult Talmud Study | Daf Yomi (Online)
events.description: Join us for a practice of reading and discussing a page of Talmud a day, led by Rabbi Carol Mitchell. A complete cycle takes seven years! We meet on-line on Friday mornings to study the most interesting and timely Talmud tractates. Daf Yomi is an international practice to read the entire Babylonian Talmud in seven and a half years at the rate of one page a day. Tens of thousands of Jews study Daf Yomi worldwide, all on the same page each day, following a schedule fixed in 1923 in Poland by Rabbi Meir Shapiro, the founder of Daf Yomi, who envisioned the whole world as a vast Talmudic classroom connected by a global network of conversational threads. Learn more Come when you can. Walk ins welcome. Prior registration is not required. If you are interested in learning with us or have questions, please contact Rabbi Carol Mitchell. All are welcome!

events.description: Welcome Shabbat with uplifting music, meaningful prayer, and the warmth of community. As we gather in the sanctuary, we create sacred space for connection, reflection, and renewal—walking together on our shared path toward shalom. This evening, we will celebrate our Havayah seniors as they mark the milestone of Confirmation, sharing their voices and receiving the blessings of our community. We will also honor Rabbi Ben Rosen and the meaningful impact he has made through his leadership, care, and presence at TBE. Honor Rabbi Rosen by making a gift to the Youth Engagement Fund. Following services, Confirmation families are invited to continue the celebration with a potluck-style dinner. We would also love to celebrate joyous shehecheyanu moments with you, from the birth of a new baby to a retirement and everything in between. events.title: American Revolution reenactment at Needham Town Common
events.description: For the third straight year, one of New England’s leading reenacting units, The King’s Own (4th Foot), is bringing its mix of history and fun to Needham Town Common May 30, 9 am-1 pm. See Redcoats drilling and preparing for war. Learn about equipment, organization, tactics, and weapons. Come face to face with soldiers sent to prevent or stop the American Revolution, and participate in an interactive debate on independence. Kids will have the chance to learn to march and handle (child safe!) muskets. Sponsored by the Needham Council for Arts and Culture.

events.title: King’s Own Come to Needham!
events.description: The British Are Coming! On Saturday, May 30th Needham will once again host The King’s Own, a group of reenactors who portray the British Redcoats who sought to prevent the American Revolution. Adults, teens, and children are invited to attend the fun, educational event that helps commemorate the 250th Anniversary of the founding of our country. See authentic uniforms, weapons, and drill, ask questions, and learn about the challenging days that led ordinary people of Needham into direct confrontation with their King and his army. Learn how British soldiers lived, fought, and made their way though seven years of warfare from Canada to the Caribbean. This event is sponsored by the Needham Cultural Council and is free to all.

events.title: Shabbat Morning Service (Hybrid)
events.description: Our Shabbat service includes the celebration of the B’Mitzvah of Solin Meyers. Registration: This event is available in person and via live stream. Registration is not required. Click here to join the live stream at the event start time. About Solin Hi everyone! My name is Solin, and I’m a 7th grader at Dover-Sherborn Middle School. My favorite subject in school is math (although recess is definitely the best!). Outside of school, I love spending time with my friends and family—especially playing with my chocolate lab puppy, Vinny. One of my favorite things to do is travel. I love exploring new places, trying new things, and going on exciting adventures. I also enjoy playing sports. Over the years, I’ve played basketball, volleyball, soccer, and softball. People always ask me which one is my favorite, but I honestly love them all. Being part of a team—supporting each other and bringing out the best in one another—makes me really happy. If you asked the people in my life to describe me, I think they would say I’m energetic, caring, and competitive. I feel so grateful for my family and friends, who make me excited to wake up each day and see what’s ahead. For my ma’asim tovim project, I wanted to focus on something meaningful to me: helping people going through difficult times. After raising nearly $2,000, I started a project called Hope for Healing. With help from the TBE community, I created comfort baskets for kids in the emergency room at local hospitals like Newton-Wellesley. I hope to continue doing projects like this, because it makes me happy to know I can make a difference in someone’s life. Becoming a bat mitzvah means more to me than just leading a service. It means becoming part of a meaningful tradition and honoring those who came before me. TBE has helped me stay connected to my Jewish identity through its activities and the strong friendships I’ve built there. I’m so excited to celebrate this special day with all of you!

events.title: Shabbat Morning Service (Hybrid)
events.description: Our Shabbat service includes the celebration of the B’Mitzvah of Emma Andler Registration: This event is available in person and via live stream. Registration is not required. Click here to join the live stream at the event start time. About Emma Hi TBE. My name is Emma Andler and I am very excited to become a bat mitzvah. I am a 7th grader at Dana Hall School where my two favorite subjects are English and Social Studies. I live in Wellesley with my Mom, Elizabeth; my Dad, Rob; my little brother Jack who is ten; my little sister Lily who is six; and our two female guinea pigs, Figgy and Zippy. My grandparents, Susan and Julian Lange, live down the street from me, and we are very close. They are long-time members of TBE and my Mom grew up going to TBE and even used to teach Hebrew School here! Some things I enjoy doing are reading, hanging out with my friends, and playing with my younger siblings. I also love horseback riding, ice skating, and Broadway musicals. Singing and playing the piano are two of my hobbies. I’ve been in some musicals and even played Anna in Frozen which was all in Hebrew!! I have also helped with costume design in some other productions, including Mean Girls, Jr. and Legally Blonde, Jr. at Dana Hall. I have gone to Grossman Camp for many years and love it there too. I am so grateful to have met so many fantastic people at TBE. I even met some of my best friends here! Leading with kindness is very important to me. Kindness is key when leading if you want a community that accepts and includes people as they are. For my bat mitzvah project, I am serving as a Madricha (teacher’s aide) for a class of kindergartners and first graders at Kesher Newton and am teaching them Hebrew and Jewish values. Once I have become a bat mitzvah, I hope to continue teaching younger kids about Judaism and life as a Jewish person and I hope to continue to stay involved in the Jewish community as well.

events.title: Mincha Ma’ariv Service (Hybrid)
events.description: Our Mincha Ma’ariv service includes the celebration of the B’Mitzvah of Avery and Marin Frieze. Registration: This event is available in person and via live stream. Registration is not required. Click here to join the live stream at the event start time. About Avery Dear TBE Community, my name is Avery Frieze. I love to play all kinds of sports, including fencing, pickleball, soccer, basketball, flag football, golf and tennis. Sports keep me active, strong and it’s fun to get to know new people. I am the youngest in my family by a minute. I have two sisters, one is my identical twin and one is my older sister. I have two parents, my mom and dad, and one absolutely adorable dog named Ottie. She is a golden retriever and I love her so much. I am in 7th grade at Carroll School, but next year I will join my sister at Beaver Country Day. I like math the best because it is one of my strengths. I have made one of my closest friends at school through my parents and TBE. I really like going to Rosh Hashana and Yom Kippur services. I love all of the singing and how everyone’s voices are so good and how everyone is synchronized. I also like the speeches that are low-key funny. Being a leader in my community makes me feel more mature and responsible. Becoming a bat mitzvah means that I am a Jewish adult and I am responsible for living with Jewish values and changing the world by helping people. About Marin Dear TBE Community, my name is Marin Frieze. I am a soccer player and I play for my school, my town and my club. My family is the best. I am an identical twin, I have an older sister, two parents and one dog. Even though I am a twin, I am technically the middle child because I was born one minute ahead of my sister. I go to Beaver Country Day School and I am in the 7th grade. I like visual arts because it’s a class where we get to make sculptures and have fun. I love hanging out with my friends and playing soccer at recess, and I like math because I think I am good at problem solving. When I think about TBE, I think about my b’mitzvah tutor, Geoffrey. He is really funny and he has made Hebrew easy and fun to learn. He has been helpful and supportive by making sure I stay on track. I am so excited to be a Jewish adult. This means a lot to me because I get to become a leader in our community and set a good example by being a good student, a person who gives back and someone who stays connected to their Judaism. For my bat mitzvah, I collected clothing to donate to Cradles to Crayons and then went and volunteered to sort clothing and make care packages with outfits for teenage girls. It was fun to create all of the different outfits and package them knowing someone would open them and be happy. I also volunteered delivering food to the elderly with Family Table.

events.description: Craft with Middle School Girl Scouts Sunday, May 31, 4:30 – 5:30 pm 5th – 7th graders snack & craft Adult orientation at 5:15 pm RSVP or ask questions: gstroop73505@gmail.com Fun & Friendship for Middle School Girls Wellesley Girl Scouts for 6th–8th graders combines fun, friendship, and adventure with community service. Scouts may also choose to pursue their Silver Award, a girl-led “take action” project which improves their community. Our troop meets monthly on Sundays late afternoon with one additional weekend activity scheduled each month. Participation is flexible based on your scout’s interests and schedule; our members attend various schools.This year’s activities have included: Community Service: archery and ice skating for younger girls, holiday party for Wellesley senior residents, and collecting food for Wellesley food Pantry. Scouts earned their animal helper badge by learning about the animals Unity Farm Sanctuary and crafting toys for Buddy Dog and Stray Pets in Need. Outings: apple picking, night climbing for Halloween, skiing at Wachusett, and back yard winter camping. The troop attended International Women’s Day at Mass Bay. We enjoyed the penguins at New England Aquarium and cannolis at Bova’s Bakery. The troop also bonded at beach volleyball, an arcade escape room, and cheered the Boston Legacy women’s soccer team to victory at Gillette Stadium. We will finish the year with a trip to Canobie Lake Park funded by our cookie sales. To RSVP or ask questions, please email gstroop73505@gmail.com Wellesley Girl Scouts are also recruiting girls at all grade levels (K-12) to form new troops or join existing troops in the fall. If you’re interested in joining Girl Scouts, please contact wellesleygs@gmail.com or customercare@gsema.org

events.title: Slavery in Massachusetts: Then, and Its Legacy Today (Hybrid)
events.description: Massachusetts is widely celebrated as the heart of the abolitionist movement—but there is a deeper, more complicated story that demands to be told. As we mark our nation’s 250th anniversary, TBE’s Racial Justice Initiative invites you to reckon with a history that is rarely acknowledged: the relationship between wealth and bondage in pre-Revolutionary Massachusetts. The Royall House and Slave Quarters in Medford was the plantation home of the Royalls—the largest slaveholding family in the Commonwealth, with at least 60 enslaved men, women, and children—existing in painful tension with Massachusetts’ Revolutionary fervor for freedom from tyranny. Joining us is Dr. Kyera Singleton, Executive Director of the Royall House and Slave Quarters, who will share the stories of those enslaved in the Greater Boston area before Massachusetts’ landmark 1783 court decision to abolish slavery. Dr. Singleton will also illuminate the remarkable resistance and activism of the enslaved and free Black people of that era—stories of courage and dignity that deserve to be heard and honored. This is history that lives in our own backyard, and as a community rooted in the values of justice, tzedakah, and repair, we are called to engage with it fully and honestly. This event is the companion experience to our June 7th tour of Royall House and Slave Quarters. Whether or not you attend that event, all are welcome to join us as we learn from Dr. Kyera Signleton. Registration: This event is available in person and online. Please click the appropriate purple bar above to register. This event is sponsored by the Racial Justice Initiative at TBE, which works to increase our understanding and activism to address ongoing discrimination and inequality.

Seated in a circle, we will play beautiful, accessible and captivating rhythms from around the world. The drumming will be enhanced with familiar chants and melodies (singing is optional). events.title: Adult Talmud Study | Daf Yomi (Online)
events.description: Join us for a practice of reading and discussing a page of Talmud a day, led by Rabbi Carol Mitchell. A complete cycle takes seven years! We meet on-line on Friday mornings to study the most interesting and timely Talmud tractates. Daf Yomi is an international practice to read the entire Babylonian Talmud in seven and a half years at the rate of one page a day. Tens of thousands of Jews study Daf Yomi worldwide, all on the same page each day, following a schedule fixed in 1923 in Poland by Rabbi Meir Shapiro, the founder of Daf Yomi, who envisioned the whole world as a vast Talmudic classroom connected by a global network of conversational threads. Learn more Come when you can. Walk ins welcome. Prior registration is not required. If you are interested in learning with us or have questions, please contact Rabbi Carol Mitchell. All are welcome!

events.title: Shabbat Morning Service (Hybrid)
events.description: Our Shabbat service includes the celebration of the B’Mitzvah of Zoe Gilman. Registration: This event is available in person and via live stream. Registration is not required. Click here to join the live stream at the event start time. About Zoe Hi Temple community, my name is Zoe. I am a 7th grader at Weston Middle School, and I am very excited to celebrate my bat mitzvah on June 6th. This is such a special milestone for me, and I feel grateful to be sharing it with my family, friends, and community. I live in Weston, Massachusetts with my family. I live with my mom and dad, my brother Gabe who is 11 years old, and my dog Griffin, who is 6 years old. My family is a really important part of my life, and I love spending time with them, whether we are at home, traveling, or doing something fun together. I love playing sports, especially soccer and swimming. Soccer is my favorite because I love being part of a team, working together, and having fun with my friends while also competing. I also really enjoy sports with my family, and one of my favorite things to do is go to Patriots games together. Those are always really fun and exciting memories for me. In the summer, I go to Camp Micah, and this will be my 5th summer there. Camp is one of my favorite places, and I love being there because I get to spend time outside, try new things, and be with my friends. I’ve made so many memories at camp, and it feels like a really special second home to me. I feel very lucky to have all of these experiences and people in my life. I am really looking forward to my bat mitzvah and everything I will learn along the way. I am excited to celebrate this day and share it with everyone who has supported me.

events.title: Bear Witness to Slavery in MA: Tour the Royall House and Slave Quarters (Offsite)
events.description: History lives in this place and we are invited to bear witness. Join the Racial Justice Initiative at TBE for a guided tour of the Royall House and Slave Quarters, the eighteenth-century plantation home of Massachusetts’ largest slaveholding family. One of the last freestanding quarters where enslaved people lived and worked in the North, this remarkable site tells the story of the Commonwealth’s unacknowledged history of slavery and how the legacy of that enslavement continues in the systemic inequities we face today. On this tour, we will hear the powerful stories of resistance and activism that led to the landmark 1783 court decision abolishing slavery in Massachusetts—stories of courage and dignity that deserve to be known and honored. The program includes: Guided tour of the Royall House and Slave Quarters: 10:00–11:30 am Post-tour discussion and lunch: 11:30 am–12:30 pm (Please bring your own lunch.) Carpooling will be available. Details to follow with registration confirmation. This visit is the companion experience to our June 3rd program with Dr. Kyera Singleton, Executive Director of the Royall House and Slave Quarters. Whether or not you attended that evening, all are welcome to join us for this extraordinary opportunity to engage with history firsthand. Audience: Adults, Youth community (middle and high school, grades 6–12) Registration: This event is available in person. Click the purple bar above to register. Sponsored by the Racial Justice Initiative at TBE, which works to deepen our understanding and activism in addressing ongoing discrimination and inequality.

events.title: “Regenerative Gardening in Today’s World”
events.description: @ Trevor Smith, an award-winning regenerative landscape designer and a principal of CFS Regenerative Design Studios, will discuss practices that climate change has brought to home gardening: planting native species to preserve biodiversity, combatting invasive species, building healthy soils, installing green infrastructure and managing water. The program will follow a very brief Friends’ Annual Meeting on June 7, 2026 at 2:00 p.m. at the Needham Free Public Library. Mr. Smith is the founder of ReEarth Solutions, a Boston-area ecological landscape design practice. He is passionate about the natural world which inspires his commitment to sustainable landscaping practices in an era of climate change. He is also passionate about sharing his knowledge and experience and is the host of the podcast “Peace, Love & Pollinators;” the live, online, “ask the expert” event Tuesdays with Trevor; and the Hive-Mind for Business Owners, a program of the Ecological Landscape Alliance. The public is invited to this free program presented by the Friends of the Needham Public Library in the Library’s Community Room at 1139 Highland Avenue, Needham, from 2-3:30 PM, Sunday, June 7. A question-and answer period will follow the presentation. Seats are limited and registration is required. Register at https://needhamlibrary.org/events/. For more information, please contact Gay Ellen Dennett, program specialist, at gdennett@minlib.net, 781-455-7559 x223. events.title: Wellesley Trails Committee Guided Walk—Centennial Reservation
events.description: Join the Wellesley Trails Committee on a guided trail walk. Free. No registration required. Walks take place unless it pours rain or there are high winds. Dogs on leashes are welcome. DATE: Saturday, June 13 TIME: 10am-11:15am LOCATION: Meet at the Mass Bay parking lot off of Oakland Street, (not the Centennial lot) DESCRIPTION: Centennial Reservation (1.8 miles). Take a picturesque walk, through rolling meadows to the top of Maugus Hill, one of the highest points in Wellesley. View the beautiful Great Blue Hills in Canton from a hillside meadow overlooking the town of Needham. Then, descend through the meadow and around Benzanson Pond, a favorite swimming hole for dogs as well as frogs and tadpoles. Come explore this conservation land purchased by the town for Wellesley’s 1981 Centennial Celebration and now one of the most popular trails in Wellesley. This walk also includes Mass Bay land that is maintained by the Wellesley Trails Committee and the Wellesley DPW.

events.title: Mincha Ma’ariv Service (Hybrid)
events.description: Our Mincha Ma’ariv service includes the celebration of the B’Mitzvah of Caroline Fishman. Registration: This event is available in person and via live stream. Registration is not required. Click here to join the live stream at the event start time. About Caroline Hi TBE Community, my name is Caroline Fishman. I live in Wellesley with my parents, Nicki and Scott, and my three younger brothers. My twin brothers, Ben and Jack, are nine, and my youngest brother, Alex, is eight. I currently attend The Meadowbrook School of Weston, where my favorite class is English because I love to read. I also enjoy tennis, skiing, baking, and especially acting in theater performances. I have been in many shows and musicals; most recently Needham Community Theatre’s production of Annie. I played the role of July, one of the orphans. I am especially looking forward to the summer, when I will be spending my fifth year at Camp Pinecliffe in Maine. I have made so many friends and connections at Temple Beth Elohim. Volunteering at Cradles to Crayons with my TBE friends has been both fun and rewarding. For my community service project, I partnered with The Soup Jar Project, a non-profit organization that helps people struggling with food insecurity by making and distributing soup jars. We held an event at my house where, with the help of friends and family, we layered dry ingredients into containers and created 204 soup jars. I loved participating in this community service project, and having my family and friends around me was such an amazing experience! As I become a Bat Mitzvah, I plan to continue to do Tikkun Olam and hopefully improve the lives of others.

events.title: TBE Music Club
events.description: Calling all music lovers! 🎶 Join us for a lively gathering where we’ll celebrate the songs you know and love, uncover new favorites, and connect with fellow music enthusiasts who share your passion. Come for the music, stay for the joy, discovery, and community! Remaining Dates: April 15: Cantor Shanna Zell will lead us through “The Journey of Women in the Reform Cantorate: Celebrating 50 Years of Sacred Leadership.” This is a program compiled by the Lowell Milken Center for Music of American Jewish Experience. May 20: Join Hal Slifer, popular WERS radio host, for Rock and Roll Memories!—a lively 60-minute multimedia journey through the music, TV Shows and Commercials, of the ’50s, ’60s, and ’70s. With rare video clips, fun backstories, and plenty of audience participation, Hal brings back the songs and moments that defined a generation. It’s a heartwarming trip down memory lane filled with laughter, sing-alongs, and great memories! June 17: A Special Afternoon with Cantors Jodi Sufrin & Roy Einhorn. Each course in this sequence is stand-alone, meaning you can attend as many or as few as you prefer! Feel free to join us for any session that interests you. If you have any questions about participating, please contact the program leader. Registration: This event is available in person and online. Please click the appropriate purple bar above to register. This event is sponsored by Music Club, where music lovers unite to learn more about music we know, discover new music, and enjoy a community of friends with musical interests. Come join us any time!

events.title: Adult Talmud Study | Daf Yomi (Online)
events.description: Join us for a practice of reading and discussing a page of Talmud a day, led by Rabbi Carol Mitchell. A complete cycle takes seven years! We meet on-line on Friday mornings to study the most interesting and timely Talmud tractates. Daf Yomi is an international practice to read the entire Babylonian Talmud in seven and a half years at the rate of one page a day. Tens of thousands of Jews study Daf Yomi worldwide, all on the same page each day, following a schedule fixed in 1923 in Poland by Rabbi Meir Shapiro, the founder of Daf Yomi, who envisioned the whole world as a vast Talmudic classroom connected by a global network of conversational threads. Learn more Come when you can. Walk ins welcome. Prior registration is not required. If you are interested in learning with us or have questions, please contact Rabbi Carol Mitchell. All are welcome!

events.description: Gather with other book lovers to discuss a book each month that is chosen a couple of months before a meeting. Our Book Club is a warm and dynamic group of people who meet to share and discuss good reads, expand their perspectives, and get to know one another through lively discussion. All are welcome at any time! Be sure to keep an eye out for our monthly newsletter with details about the upcoming book and its author. If you are not on our Book Club list, and would like to be added, please reach out to Lynn Burke Harrell, especially if you would like to participate via Zoom. That way, you will receive the emails with the Zoom information for each meeting. Each course in this sequence is stand-alone, meaning you can attend as many or as few as you prefer! Feel free to join us for any session that interests you. If you have any questions about participating, please contact the program leader. September 16, 2025: The Love Elixir of Augusta Stern by Lynda Cohen Loigman (fiction) October 21, 2025: Treyf: My Life as an Unorthodox Outlaw by Elissa Altman (memoir) November 18, 2025: The One Man by Andrew Gross (fiction) January 6, 2026: The Last Kings of Shanghai: The Rival Jewish Dynasties that Helped Create China by Jonathan Kaufman (non-fiction) January 20, 2026: The Amen Effect: Ancient Wisdom to Mend Our Broken Hearts and World by Sharon Brous (non-fiction) February 17, 2026: Goyhood by Reuven Fenton (fiction) Book Club will be held In Person only for the February meeting. March 17, 2026: Fatherland: A Memoir of War, Conscience, and Family Secrets by Burkhard Bilger (memoir) April 28, 2026: The Boston Girl. by Anita Diamant (fiction) May 19, 2026: The Year of Living Biblically: One Man’s Humble Quest to Follow The Bible as Literally as Possible by AJ Jacobs (non-fiction) To RSVP, or if you have any questions, please be in touch with Trina Reinach at sreinach@rcn.com. events.title: Adult Talmud Study | Daf Yomi (Online)
events.description: Join us for a practice of reading and discussing a page of Talmud a day, led by Rabbi Carol Mitchell. A complete cycle takes seven years! We meet on-line on Friday mornings to study the most interesting and timely Talmud tractates. Daf Yomi is an international practice to read the entire Babylonian Talmud in seven and a half years at the rate of one page a day. Tens of thousands of Jews study Daf Yomi worldwide, all on the same page each day, following a schedule fixed in 1923 in Poland by Rabbi Meir Shapiro, the founder of Daf Yomi, who envisioned the whole world as a vast Talmudic classroom connected by a global network of conversational threads. Learn more Come when you can. Walk ins welcome. Prior registration is not required. If you are interested in learning with us or have questions, please contact Rabbi Carol Mitchell. All are welcome!

TBE’s open ceramics studio is your invitation to slow down, get your hands a little messy, and create alongside friends new and old. Whether you show up for the full session or drop in for an hour or two, you’ll find a relaxed, welcoming space where all skill levels are genuinely celebrated. Each session draws inspiration from Torah texts, weaving learning and creativity into one experience. A trained ceramics guide will be there to support your journey every step of the way—from your first coil to your finished glaze. What to expect: July 8 & 15—Handbuilding and finishing techniques July 29—Glazing only All pieces will be fired professionally in TBE’s own kiln. Snacks and drinks included. A note on sessions: Because ceramics unfolds in stages—building, firing, glazing—we’re unable to offer individual session pricing. Each step is part of the whole. Come make something. Come connect. Come find a little shalom at the wheel. Registration: This event is available in person. Please click the purple bar above to register. events.title: “People”hood of the Book (Online)
events.description: Join Rabbi Jordy Callman for a summer reading series that brings fresh, vibrant voices to the table. Together, we’ll journey through three novels by Jewish authors—all published within the last year—each one illuminating a different facet of Jewish identity, memory, and belonging. Come for one session or all three. Every gathering stands on its own. July 9 — This Is Not About Us by Allegra Goodman July 16 — The Spoon and the Sea by Rachel Caplin July 23 — Sisters of Fortune by Esther Chehebar No homework required—just an open mind, a love of story, and curiosity about what it means to be part of this remarkable people. Each course in this sequence is stand-alone, meaning you can attend as many or as few as you prefer! Feel free to join us for any session that interests you. If you have any questions about participating, please contact the program leader. Registration: This event is available online. Please click the appropriate purple bar above to register. This event is a part of Summer Institute 2026: Stories That Shape Us, a month of immersive experiences centered on the creation of our community Torah scroll, Scribing Our Story. Offerings include hands-on workshops, text study, and conversations with educators and clergy. Engage at TBE, online, and beyond our walls, while deepening your connection to Jewish tradition and discovering your place in the Jewish story! Click here to view a list of all Summer Institute programs. We are deeply grateful to the family of Burton D. “Bud” Rose, M.D., who has underwritten the Summer Institute in loving memory of Bud, who was a lifelong teacher, learner, and lover of Jewish community.

events.title: Finding Ourselves in Tanakh (In Person)
events.description: At the Beginning of Our Story: Finding Ourselves in Tanakh Where did we come from—and who were we meant to become? This captivating journey through Torah, Prophets, and Writings invites us to explore the very roots of our identity as a people. Together, we’ll trace how our ancestors found their way to monotheism, how Torah became more than a text — a living blueprint for a life of goodness and purpose—and what it meant when we strayed from the covenant forged at Sinai. Along the way, we’ll meet the ordinary men and women who answered an extraordinary call. Their courage, their stumbles, and their resilience echo across the centuries, speaking directly to our own search for meaning, wholeness, and belonging. Because the stories of Tanakh aren’t only about then. They’re about us—rooted in tradition, still reaching toward shalom. All our paths lead home. This is where the journey begins. Dates: July 10, 17, 24 Registration:This event is available in person. Please click the purple bar above to register. This event is a part of Summer Institute 2026: Stories That Shape Us, a month of immersive experiences centered on the creation of our community Torah scroll, Scribing Our Story. Offerings include hands-on workshops, text study, and conversations with educators and clergy. Engage at TBE, online, and beyond our walls, while deepening your connection to Jewish tradition and discovering your place in the Jewish story! events.title: Life Review Workshop (In Person)
events.description: “They Are Our Life and the Length of Our Days” (from the Aahavat Olam prayer) Every life is a Torah—full of chapters worth revisiting, wisdom worth sharing, and meaning still unfolding. Rooted in the insight of psychologist Erik Erikson, who believed our deepest work in older adulthood is gathering our experiences into a coherent narrative, this workshop offers something rare and precious: dedicated time and sacred space to reflect on the life you’ve lived. Through conversation, creative exploration, and the grounding wisdom of Jewish texts, we’ll weave together memory and meaning—looking honestly at the path we’ve walked, the moment we’re standing in, and the road still ahead. This is more than reminiscence. It’s an act of wholeness. Come ready to listen deeply, speak wholeheartedly, and discover the Torah that only you can teach. All our paths lead to shalom—including the one that brought you here. Dates: Mondays, July 13, 20 & 27 | 11:00 am—12:30 pm Registration: This event is available in person. Please click on the purple button above to register.

events.title: Finding Ourselves in Tanakh (In Person)
events.description: At the Beginning of Our Story: Finding Ourselves in Tanakh Where did we come from—and who were we meant to become? This captivating journey through Torah, Prophets, and Writings invites us to explore the very roots of our identity as a people. Together, we’ll trace how our ancestors found their way to monotheism, how Torah became more than a text — a living blueprint for a life of goodness and purpose—and what it meant when we strayed from the covenant forged at Sinai. Along the way, we’ll meet the ordinary men and women who answered an extraordinary call. Their courage, their stumbles, and their resilience echo across the centuries, speaking directly to our own search for meaning, wholeness, and belonging. Because the stories of Tanakh aren’t only about then. They’re about us—rooted in tradition, still reaching toward shalom. All our paths lead home. This is where the journey begins. Dates: July 10, 17, 24 Registration:This event is available in person. Please click the purple bar above to register. This event is a part of Summer Institute 2026: Stories That Shape Us, a month of immersive experiences centered on the creation of our community Torah scroll, Scribing Our Story. Offerings include hands-on workshops, text study, and conversations with educators and clergy. Engage at TBE, online, and beyond our walls, while deepening your connection to Jewish tradition and discovering your place in the Jewish story! Click here to view a list of all Summer Institute programs. We are deeply grateful to the family of Burton D. “Bud” Rose, M.D., who has underwritten the Summer Institute in loving memory of Bud, who was a lifelong teacher, learner, and lover of Jewish community.

events.title: Life Review Workshop (In Person)
events.description: “They Are Our Life and the Length of Our Days” (from the Aahavat Olam prayer) Every life is a Torah—full of chapters worth revisiting, wisdom worth sharing, and meaning still unfolding. Rooted in the insight of psychologist Erik Erikson, who believed our deepest work in older adulthood is gathering our experiences into a coherent narrative, this workshop offers something rare and precious: dedicated time and sacred space to reflect on the life you’ve lived. Through conversation, creative exploration, and the grounding wisdom of Jewish texts, we’ll weave together memory and meaning—looking honestly at the path we’ve walked, the moment we’re standing in, and the road still ahead. This is more than reminiscence. It’s an act of wholeness. Come ready to listen deeply, speak wholeheartedly, and discover the Torah that only you can teach. All our paths lead to shalom—including the one that brought you here. Dates: Mondays, July 13, 20 & 27 | 11:00 am—12:30 pm Registration: This event is available in person. Please click on the purple button above to register.

events.title: “People”hood of the Book (Online)
events.description: Join Rabbi Jordy Callman for a summer reading series that brings fresh, vibrant voices to the table. Together, we’ll journey through three novels by Jewish authors—all published within the last year—each one illuminating a different facet of Jewish identity, memory, and belonging. Come for one session or all three. Every gathering stands on its own. July 9 — This Is Not About Us by Allegra Goodman July 16 — The Spoon and the Sea by Rachel Caplin July 23 — Sisters of Fortune by Esther Chehebar No homework required—just an open mind, a love of story, and curiosity about what it means to be part of this remarkable people. Each course in this sequence is stand-alone, meaning you can attend as many or as few as you prefer! Feel free to join us for any session that interests you. If you have any questions about participating, please contact the program leader. Registration: This event is available online. Please click the appropriate purple bar above to register. events.description: Framingham-based artist Ileana Doble Hernandez invites you to reflect on the use of collage as a way to resist and explore our current socio-political situation, as well as a form of personal portrayal. You’ll have time to create a small collage piece! We’ll provide blank paper, scissors, and magazines for clipping. You’re also welcome to bring your own magazines. Other materials like newspapers, discarded books, or copies of photographs also make great sources for collage.

events.title: Torah Text & Texture: Summer Ceramics Open Studios (In Person)
events.description: Create, Connect, and Shape Something Beautiful There’s something deeply meditative about working with clay—and something equally nourishing about doing it in good company. TBE’s open ceramics studio is your invitation to slow down, get your hands a little messy, and create alongside friends new and old. Whether you show up for the full session or drop in for an hour or two, you’ll find a relaxed, welcoming space where all skill levels are genuinely celebrated. Each session draws inspiration from Torah texts, weaving learning and creativity into one experience. A trained ceramics guide will be there to support your journey every step of the way—from your first coil to your finished glaze. What to expect: July 8 & 15—Handbuilding and finishing techniques July 29—Glazing only All pieces will be fired professionally in TBE’s own kiln. Snacks and drinks included. A note on sessions: Because ceramics unfolds in stages—building, firing, glazing—we’re unable to offer individual session pricing. Each step is part of the whole. Come make something. Come connect. Come find a little shalom at the wheel. Registration: This event is available in person. Please click the purple bar above to register. This program is part of The Well at TBE, Institute for Integrative Health, Wellness, and Spirituality. The Well at TBE integrates Jewish spiritual practices and modern mindfulness tools and techniques to provide a source of wellbeing through learning, practice, prayer, and support for individuals on their own and in community.

events.title: Call for Walkers: Register for the 38th Annual Jimmy Fund Walk for Dana-Farber presented by Hyundai
events.description: Registration is open for the 2026 Jimmy Fund Walk for Dana-Farber presented by Hyundai. Scheduled for Sunday, Oct. 4, funds raised from the Walk support all forms of adult and pediatric care and cancer research at Dana-Farber Cancer Institute. The 2026 event will aim to raise $10 million in support of Dana-Farber’s mission to defy cancer by leading the way in groundbreaking research and lifesaving patient care. The Details: The Jimmy Fund Walk is the only organized walk permitted to use the famed Boston Marathon® course, and participants have the flexibility to choose from four distance options: 5K walk: Starting at Dana-Farber’s Longwood Medical Campus 10K walk: Starting in Newton Half Marathon walk: Starting in Wellesley Marathon walk: Starting in Hopkinton Along the route, participants will be motivated by Hero posters, photos and quotes from Dana-Farber patients, who are considered the heart and inspiration for the event. Walkers will also enjoy refreshments and cheer squads throughout the course. The event will conclude at the Boston Common Finish Line, by the corner of Charles and Beacon Streets. Finish line activities will include a celebration with food, entertainment, and more. Walkers who prefer to participate virtually can complete their chosen distance from any location. To register for the Jimmy Fund Walk for Dana-Farber or to support a walker, visit JimmyFund.org/Walk or call (866) 531-9255. Registrants can enter the promo code NEWS for $5 off the registration fee. You can also sign up to volunteer on Walk Day.

events.title: Civil Rights Journey 2026
events.description: Unanimously described as a transformative experience by the more than 200 TBE congregants who have traveled together on five previous Civil Rights Journeys, we are thrilled to announce another trip — and we hope you’ll join us. From October 22–25, 2026, we will travel together to Atlanta, Montgomery, Selma, and Birmingham, exploring not only the poignant history of the Civil Rights Movement but also the continuing and deeply relevant legacy of slavery, Reconstruction, Jim Crow, and mass incarceration in America today. This journey is a profound expression of TBE’s commitment to racial justice — a chance to bear witness, to learn, and to recommit ourselves to the work of building a more just world. Highlights of the journey include: Rosa Parks Museum Equal Justice Initiative’s Legacy Museum, the National Memorial for Peace and Justice (Lynching Memorial), and the Freedom Sculpture Garden Birmingham Civil Rights Institute The King Center Shabbat services at Montgomery’s Temple Beth Or Sunday morning services at Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.’s Ebenezer Baptist Church in Atlanta The trip is coordinated by ETGAR 36 (etgar.org), an Atlanta-based organization specializing in Civil Rights Tours for synagogues and Jewish youth groups. What’s included: All meals (Friday morning breakfast through Sunday morning breakfast — 7 meals in total), all ground transportation, hotel accommodations, and all admission and programming costs. Flights to and from Atlanta are not included. Frequently Asked Questions and Itinerary Registration: This event is available in person. Please click the purple bar above to register. Deposits due May 31. Registration closes and final payment due June 30. This event is sponsored by the Racial Justice Initiative at TBE, which works to increase our understanding and activism to address ongoing discrimination and inequality.

events.title: A Tapestry of Faith: A Jewish Journey Through Morocco (In Person)
events.description: Embark on a meaningful journey through Morocco that reflects Temple Beth Elohim’s commitment to lifelong learning, shared experience, and the exploration of Jewish life in all its diversity. Together, we will travel through a land where centuries of Jewish history are interwoven with vibrant cultures, faith traditions, and enduring relationships across communities. As we explore Morocco’s ancient medinas, historic mellahs (Jewish quarters), and beautifully preserved synagogues and cemeteries, we will encounter stories of resilience, creativity, and coexistence that echo TBE’s values of connection, curiosity, and respect for difference. From Fez and Marrakech to Rabat and Casablanca, we’ll learn not only about the past, but also about contemporary Jewish life and the ways tradition continues to adapt and thrive. Guided by Rabbi Joel Sisenwine and enriched by scholarly insight and shared reflection, this journey invites us to engage deeply—with place, with people, and with one another. Along the way, we will experience the warmth of Moroccan hospitality, celebrate Shabbat in community, and open ourselves to moments of learning, meaning, and spiritual discovery. This is more than a tour; it is an opportunity to travel as a TBE community—strengthening relationships, expanding perspectives, and returning home inspired by the enduring legacy of Jewish life and the power of cultural exchange. Registration: This event takes place in person. Click the purple bar to learn more & join us.

events.title: Art & the Family Archive | The Persistence of Family with Diana Cheren Nygren
events.description: Join Diana Cheren Nygren for a talk about breathing new life into the family archive at a time when photographs are everywhere but the traditional family photo album is disappearing. What can we learn about ourselves from our history? It is a well known cliche that ancestors worked and acted in hopes of building a better future for their descendants. But what does this mean once the possibility of a better future all but disappears? Does that change our relationship to the past as well as the future?

events.title: Adult Talmud Study | Daf Yomi (Online)
events.description: Join us for a practice of reading and discussing a page of Talmud a day, led by Rabbi Carol Mitchell. A complete cycle takes seven years! We meet on-line on Friday mornings to study the most interesting and timely Talmud tractates. Daf Yomi is an international practice to read the entire Babylonian Talmud in seven and a half years at the rate of one page a day. Tens of thousands of Jews study Daf Yomi worldwide, all on the same page each day, following a schedule fixed in 1923 in Poland by Rabbi Meir Shapiro, the founder of Daf Yomi, who envisioned the whole world as a vast Talmudic classroom connected by a global network of conversational threads. Learn more Come when you can. Walk ins welcome. Prior registration is not required. If you are interested in learning with us or have questions, please contact Rabbi Carol Mitchell. All are welcome!

events.title: Hike Through History – Callahan State Park
events.description: As the largest green space in Framingham, Callahan State Park is a year-round destination for hiking, birdwatching, horseback riding, mountain biking, and cross-country skiing. Yet before it became a state park more than 50 years ago, the land had been farmland for centuries. What traces of that history remain, and what stories do they tell?

events.title: Revolutionary Networks: Printers, Politics, and the Power of the Press
events.description: Join historian and author Joseph M. Adelman for an insightful exploration of the vital role newspaper printers played during the American Revolution. Based on his acclaimed book, Revolutionary Networks, this lecture delves into the fascinating world of colonial print culture and its impact on shaping Revolutionary political ideology and mass mobilization.

events.title: Art & the Family Archive: “Photography and Words – An Ekphrastic Poetry Experience”
events.description: Join artist and writer Julie Williams-Krishnan for an engaging and immersive workshop exploring the connection between photography and words through the art of ekphrastic poetry. Hosted at the Framingham History Center, this interactive session invites participants to reflect on historical and contemporary photographs as inspiration for creative writing.

Daf Yomi is an international practice to read the entire Babylonian Talmud in seven and a half years at the rate of one page a day. Tens of thousands of Jews study Daf Yomi worldwide, all on the same page each day, following a schedule fixed in 1923 in Poland by Rabbi Meir Shapiro, the founder of Daf Yomi, who envisioned the whole world as a vast Talmudic classroom connected by a global network of conversational threads. Learn more Come when you can. Walk ins welcome. Prior registration is not required. If you are interested in learning with us or have questions, please contact Rabbi Carol Mitchell. All are welcome!
